title: Gsuite suspicious calendar invite
spl:
`gsuite_calendar`
      | bin span=5m _time
      | rename parameters.* as *
      | search target_calendar_id!=null email="*yourdomain.com"
      | stats  count values(target_calendar_id) values(event_title) values(event_guest)
        BY email _time
      | where count >100
      | `gsuite_suspicious_calendar_invite_filter`
description: The following analytic detects suspicious calendar invites sent via GSuite, potentially indicating compromised accounts or malicious internal activity. It leverages GSuite calendar logs, focusing on events where a high volume of invites (over 100) is sent within a 5-minute window. This behavior is significant as it may involve the distribution of malicious links or attachments, posing a security risk. If confirmed malicious, this activity could lead to widespread phishing attacks, unauthorized a…

implementation: ESCU Hunting detection — not designed for automated alerting. Run ad-hoc or on a low-frequency schedule from Splunk Enterprise Security for analyst-driven investigation. Requires Various ingested and CIM-normalized.
knownFalsePositives: Conference and recruiting calendar invites with external co-organizers. Verify the organizer identity and calendar sharing settings before closing as malicious.
detailedImplementation:
Enterprise Security Detection Rule

"Gsuite suspicious calendar invite" is a Hunting detection designed for analyst-driven threat hunting rather than automated alerting, sourced from the Splunk Enterprise Security Content Update (ESCU). Hunting detections are not intended for automated alerting. They are run on-demand or on a low-frequency schedule by security analysts investigating specific threat hypotheses.

Prerequisites

• Splunk Enterprise Security 7.x or later with the ES Content Update (ESCU) app installed and up to date.
• Data sources: Various. Must be ingested into Splunk and normalized to the Common Information Model (CIM) via the appropriate Technology Add-on.
• MITRE ATT&CK: T1566. Review the ATT&CK matrix for adjacent techniques to identify detection coverage gaps in your environment.

Deployment

1. In Enterprise Security, navigate to Configure → Content → Content Management.
2. Search for "Gsuite suspicious calendar invite" or filter by Analytic Story.
3. Hunting detections are typically left disabled for automated scheduling. Instead, run them on-demand from the Search bar or schedule at low frequency (daily or weekly).
4. Review results manually — hunting detections cast a wider net and expect analyst judgment to separate signal from noise.
5. When results warrant further investigation, create a Notable Event manually or initiate your incident response workflow.

Tuning and False Positive Management

Known false positives for this detection: This search will also produce normal activity statistics. Fields such as email, ip address, name, parameters.organizer_calendar_id, parameters.target_calendar_id and parameters.event_title may give away phishing intent.For more specific results use email parameter.

• Hunting detections are expected to produce broader result sets that require analyst interpretation. Focus on refining the search scope (time range, specific hosts or users) rather than eliminating all noise.
• Maintain a hunting journal documenting hypotheses tested, results found, and detection improvements identified.
• Share high-fidelity findings with the detection engineering team to convert recurring hunting patterns into automated TTP or Anomaly detections.

Analyst Response Workflow

When reviewing Hunting results:

1. Run the search manually or review scheduled results. Evaluate each returned event against your threat hypothesis — not every result indicates compromise.
2. Cross-reference findings with current threat intelligence, recent security advisories, and outputs from other detection sources.
3. If suspicious activity is confirmed, create a Notable Event or escalate directly to your incident response process with the supporting evidence.
4. Document the hunting exercise: hypothesis, data sources queried, time range, findings, and any detection engineering improvements identified.

Validation

Compare results in Splunk with the Microsoft 365 Defender portal (https://security.microsoft.com) for M365- and cloud identity–backed content; use the Proofpoint TAP dashboard for Proofpoint, the Mimecast Personal Portal for Mimecast, and Cisco ESA `mail_logs` and Message Tracking for on-prem gateways.

Confirm the required data sources are flowing:

```spl
| tstats count where index=* by index, sourcetype | sort -count
```

For ESCU correlation searches, verify the search is enabled and recent fires appear as expected:

```spl
index=_audit action="alert_fired" ss_name="*"
| stats count by ss_name, trigger_time | sort -trigger_time
```
grandmaExplanation: We watch mail flow, attachments, and policy outcomes in normalized email data, so threats and data-loss events tied to messages are easier to see across gateways and inboxes in one place.
cimSpl:
| tstats `summariesonly` count
  from datamodel=Email.All_Email
  by All_Email.src_user All_Email.recipient span=1h
| sort -count
dataModelAcceleration: Enable Data Model Acceleration for the CIM `Email.All_Email` dataset once message and attachment fields are CIM-tagged; otherwise `cimSpl` tstats may return no rows.
